title: Basis of payment - Price and currency adjustments | CanadaBuys

structure_blocks.heading: Buyer's Guide Basis of payment - Price and currency adjustments
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Basis of payment - Price and currency adjustments
structure_blocks.text: Price and currency adjustments Economic price adjustments (EPAs) and foreign currency adjustments (FCAs) are tools that help manage pricing risks from market fluctuations beyond the control of Canada and the contractor. This includes changes in commodity prices, foreign exchange or labour rates subject to collective bargaining. Usage guidelines These tools are used when Canada and the contractor cannot estimate future costs of materials, labour or overhead accurately, and where these costs could change significantly. You can use them for both competitive and non-competitive contracts with a fixed price or fixed time/unit rate basis of payment. Adjustments to fixed prices or fixed time/unit prices in a contract are only allowed if provided for in the contract. Include the appropriate clauses for EPAs or FCAs in the contract. Economic price adjustments EPAs should not normally be included in contracts with delivery schedules under 12 months or in contracts valued below $100,000. Any price adjustment formula within a contract must accommodate both increases and decreases in the established firm base price. The formula for adjustments must detail: any economic wage or price index to be used the firm price element the base period for which adjustments will be made either a ceiling price or a limitation of expenditure The calculation of any adjustment formula should be consistent with the cost or price accounting treatment used to set the firm base price. This consistency ensures that variations from the firm base price are accurately measured. The price adjustment formula should be the simplest, most suitable method that minimizes administrative burdens while protecting the interests of both contracting parties. You should consult a cost analyst to: develop significant EPA provisions implement an EPA provision using an accounting type formula For a competitive solicitation of offers, the proposed EPA provisions must be considered in the evaluation of the offers. In all other situations, you must negotiate these provisions during the initial or base year contract price discussions. For commercial goods and services, adjustment provisions should be based on changes from an agreed posted reference or firm base price. If the original contract or firm base price includes a discount factor, the same discount factor should be applied to the adjusted price, unless the contract specifies otherwise. Statistics Canada and similar entities such as the United States Department of Labor publish data on changes in price indices, and material and labour costs. Private sector surveys may also be used. Adjustments to actual rates for labour or actual costs for material are based on the increases or decreases in firm base price elements experienced by the contractor. The use of this adjustment method is limited to contingencies beyond the contractor’s control, and where the contractor's accounting system permits timely gathering and processing of relevant, supporting cost data. A company's union agreement with its employees may serve as a valid index for labour rate adjustments if it reflects comparable wage trends within that industry. Alternatives There are alternatives to using EPAs and FCAs . For more information refer to the EPAs and FCAs section 4.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing .

raw_text:
Price and currency adjustments Economic price adjustments (EPAs) and foreign currency adjustments (FCAs) are tools that help manage pricing risks from market fluctuations beyond the control of Canada and the contractor. This includes changes in commodity prices, foreign exchange or labour rates subject to collective bargaining. Usage guidelines These tools are used when Canada and the contractor cannot estimate future costs of materials, labour or overhead accurately, and where these costs could change significantly. You can use them for both competitive and non-competitive contracts with a fixed price or fixed time/unit rate basis of payment. Adjustments to fixed prices or fixed time/unit prices in a contract are only allowed if provided for in the contract. Include the appropriate clauses for EPAs or FCAs in the contract. Economic price adjustments EPAs should not normally be included in contracts with delivery schedules under 12 months or in contracts valued below $100,000. Any price adjustment formula within a contract must accommodate both increases and decreases in the established firm base price. The formula for adjustments must detail: any economic wage or price index to be used the firm price element the base period for which adjustments will be made either a ceiling price or a limitation of expenditure The calculation of any adjustment formula should be consistent with the cost or price accounting treatment used to set the firm base price. This consistency ensures that variations from the firm base price are accurately measured. The price adjustment formula should be the simplest, most suitable method that minimizes administrative burdens while protecting the interests of both contracting parties. You should consult a cost analyst to: develop significant EPA provisions implement an EPA provision using an accounting type formula For a competitive solicitation of offers, the proposed EPA provisions must be considered in the evaluation of the offers. In all other situations, you must negotiate these provisions during the initial or base year contract price discussions. For commercial goods and services, adjustment provisions should be based on changes from an agreed posted reference or firm base price. If the original contract or firm base price includes a discount factor, the same discount factor should be applied to the adjusted price, unless the contract specifies otherwise. Statistics Canada and similar entities such as the United States Department of Labor publish data on changes in price indices, and material and labour costs. Private sector surveys may also be used. Adjustments to actual rates for labour or actual costs for material are based on the increases or decreases in firm base price elements experienced by the contractor. The use of this adjustment method is limited to contingencies beyond the contractor’s control, and where the contractor's accounting system permits timely gathering and processing of relevant, supporting cost data. A company's union agreement with its employees may serve as a valid index for labour rate adjustments if it reflects comparable wage trends within that industry. Alternatives There are alternatives to using EPAs and FCAs . For more information refer to the EPAs and FCAs section 4.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing .

Price and currency adjustments

Economic price adjustments (EPAs) and foreign currency adjustments (FCAs) are tools that help manage pricing risks from market fluctuations beyond the control of Canada and the contractor. This includes changes in commodity prices, foreign exchange or labour rates subject to collective bargaining.

Usage guidelines

These tools are used when Canada and the contractor cannot estimate future costs of materials, labour or overhead accurately, and where these costs could change significantly. You can use them for both competitive and non-competitive contracts with a fixed price or fixed time/unit rate basis of payment.

Adjustments to fixed prices or fixed time/unit prices in a contract are only allowed if provided for in the contract. Include the appropriate clauses for EPAs or FCAs in the contract.

Economic price adjustments

EPAs should not normally be included in contracts with delivery schedules under 12 months or in contracts valued below $100,000. Any price adjustment formula within a contract must accommodate both increases and decreases in the established firm base price.

The formula for adjustments must detail:

any economic wage or price index to be used

the firm price element

the base period for which adjustments will be made

either a ceiling price or a limitation of expenditure

The calculation of any adjustment formula should be consistent with the cost or price accounting treatment used to set the firm base price. This consistency ensures that variations from the firm base price are accurately measured. The price adjustment formula should be the simplest, most suitable method that minimizes administrative burdens while protecting the interests of both contracting parties.

You should consult a cost analyst to:

develop significant EPA provisions

implement an EPA provision using an accounting type formula

For a competitive solicitation of offers, the proposed EPA provisions must be considered in the evaluation of the offers. In all other situations, you must negotiate these provisions during the initial or base year contract price discussions.

For commercial goods and services, adjustment provisions should be based on changes from an agreed posted reference or firm base price. If the original contract or firm base price includes a discount factor, the same discount factor should be applied to the adjusted price, unless the contract specifies otherwise.

Statistics Canada and similar entities such as the United States Department of Labor publish data on changes in price indices, and material and labour costs. Private sector surveys may also be used.

Adjustments to actual rates for labour or actual costs for material are based on the increases or decreases in firm base price elements experienced by the contractor. The use of this adjustment method is limited to contingencies beyond the contractor’s control, and where the contractor's accounting system permits timely gathering and processing of relevant, supporting cost data.

A company's union agreement with its employees may serve as a valid index for labour rate adjustments if it reflects comparable wage trends within that industry.

Alternatives

There are alternatives to using EPAs and FCAs . For more information refer to the EPAs and FCAs section 4.2 of the Practitioner’s Guide for Procurement Pricing .
